title: Dog Walking Wobbly Like Drunk? Causes and Emergency Steps
meta_desc: Is your dog walking wobbly or off-balance? Learn why this drunken gait (ataxia) is a high-urgency veterinary emergency and how to help.
content_html:
TL;DR: Ataxia, or wobbly walking, is a veterinary emergency that requires immediate medical attention to rule out serious issues like toxin ingestion or neurological damage. Recording a video of your dog’s gait can significantly assist the vet in making a faster, more accurate diagnosis.

What is ataxia and why is my dog walking unsteadily?

  • Ataxia refers to wobbly, unsteady walking caused by a lack of muscle coordination.
  • You may notice your dog stumbling, swaying, or crossing their legs while moving.
  • The condition can be caused by dysfunction in the inner ear (vestibular system), spinal cord, or brain.

Is wobbly walking in my dog a veterinary emergency?

  • Yes, sudden wobbly walking is a high-urgency situation that requires immediate professional evaluation.
  • It can be a symptom of toxin ingestion, strokes, severe inner ear infections, or spinal injuries.
  • Prompt treatment is necessary because these conditions can progress rapidly or lead to permanent damage.

How can recording a video of my dog's movement help the vet?

  • A video provides an objective look at the dog's movement, which is helpful because dogs often mask symptoms in a clinic.
  • It helps the veterinary team differentiate between vestibular disease, general weakness, and neurological deficits.
  • This visual evidence allows for a faster diagnosis and a more efficient treatment plan.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Cerebellar disorders, brain stem disorders, spinal cord disorders, and neuromuscular disorders can cause incoordination and gait abnormalities in dogs. Cerebellar disorders typically result in an intention tremor, wide-based stance, and dysmetria of the head, trunk, and limbs. Brain stem disorders may result in cranial nerve dysfunction, postural disturbances, or vestibular dysfunction and can also cause weakness and proprioceptive gait disturbances. Spinal cord disorders produce weakness, motor dysfunction, or proprioceptive deficits of the limbs, including dysmetria or reduced proprioceptive placing in more than one limb, but do not affect cerebral function or head movement coordination. Alcohol intoxication can also cause similar signs; severely affected animals may exhibit ataxia, tremors, dyspnea, progressing to coma, hypothermia, seizures, bradycardia, and respiratory depression, potentially leading to death. Initial emergency steps for alcohol intoxication involve stabilizing the animal, maintaining adequate ventilation, correcting cardiovascular and acid-base abnormalities, and controlling seizures with diazepam (0.5-2 mg/kg, IV) as needed. Induction of emesis may be beneficial in asymptomatic animals within 20-40 minutes of ingestion, and bathing is recommended for dermal exposures.

Chapter: Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1226)

faqs.question: Can a dog recover from walking wobbly?
faqs.answer: Recovery depends entirely on the underlying cause. Conditions like Vestibular Disease often improve with time, while toxin ingestion or spinal issues require immediate medical intervention for a positive prognosis.

faqs.question: What toxins cause a dog to act drunk?
faqs.answer: Common household toxins include xylitol (found in sugar-free gum), certain human medications, macadamia nuts, and marijuana. Always tell your vet if your dog could have accessed any of these.

faqs.question: Should I wait to see if it gets better?
faqs.answer: No. Because ataxia can be a sign of a life-threatening emergency or severe pain, you should seek veterinary care immediately rather than waiting for symptoms to resolve on their own.
